header.title: Heap
header.description: A heap is a complete binary tree stored as a flat array where every parent satisfies an ordering property with its children. It powers priority queues — giving O(1) access to the minimum or maximum and O(log n) insert and extract.

howItWorks.title: How heaps work
items: A heap is stored as a level-order array — no pointers needed. Parent of index i is at ⌊(i-1)/2⌋, left child at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2. All navigation is arithmetic, not pointer chasing.
Min-heap property: every parent ≤ its children. Max-heap property: every parent ≥ its children. Only the root is guaranteed to be the global min/max — the rest are partially ordered.
Insert appends the new element at the end (O(1)) then bubbles it up by repeatedly swapping with its parent until the property is restored. At most ⌊log₂n⌋ swaps — O(log n).
Extract removes the root, moves the last element to index 0, then sifts it down by swapping with the smaller (min-heap) or larger (max-heap) child until the property is restored. O(log n).

Heapify builds a valid heap from an arbitrary array in O(n) — not O(n log n). The key insight: sift-down only internal nodes (indices 0 to ⌊n/2⌋-1) bottom-up. Leaf nodes are already trivially valid heaps.
Delete at index and Update key both run in O(log n): replace the target, then apply exactly one of bubble-up or sift-down — never both.

variants.label: Min-Heap
variants.description: Every parent is ≤ its children. The root is always the global minimum. Used in Dijkstra's algorithm, Prim's MST, and the merge k sorted lists problem.
characteristics: Insert: append + bubble-up comparing child < parent
Extract min: remove root, move last to root, sift-down comparing parent > smaller child
Used in priority queues where lowest priority number = highest priority

variants.label: Max-Heap
variants.description: Every parent is ≥ its children. The root is always the global maximum. Used in heap sort and top-K element problems.
characteristics: Insert: append + bubble-up comparing child > parent
Extract max: remove root, move last to root, sift-down comparing parent < larger child
Java PriorityQueue is min-heap by default — use reverseOrder() for max-heap

variants.label: Binary vs d-ary Heap
variants.structure:
Binary (d=2):  parent(i) = ⌊(i-1)/2⌋
Ternary (d=3): parent(i) = ⌊(i-1)/3⌋
d-ary:         children at d×i+1 … d×i+d
variants.description: A d-ary heap has d children per node instead of 2. Larger d means shallower tree (log_d n height) so insert is faster, but extract is slower because more children must be compared at each level.
characteristics: Binary heap (d=2): insert O(log₂n), extract O(2·log₂n) — the standard choice
4-ary heap: better cache performance due to fewer levels — used in practice
Fibonacci heap: amortized O(1) decrease-key — used in advanced Dijkstra
d-ary heaps reduce memory indirection in high-performance implementations

variants.label: Why Heapify is O(n)
variants.structure:
Naive (n inserts):  O(n log n)
Heapify (bottom-up): O(n)

Height h has ⌈n/2^(h+1)⌉ nodes.
Work at height h = O(h).
Total = Σ ⌈n/2^(h+1)⌉·h = O(n).
variants.description: Building a heap by inserting n elements one by one costs O(n log n). Bottom-up heapify costs O(n) — a factor of log n better. The trick: most nodes are near the bottom where sift paths are short.
characteristics: Half the nodes are leaves — zero sift-down work (already valid)
Quarter of nodes are at height 1 — at most 1 swap each
The geometric series Σ h/2^h converges to 2 — total work is 2n = O(n)
Critical for heap sort and building priority queues from bulk data
useCases: Building median-stream heaps from existing data

files.code:
# ─── Heap in Python ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#
# Python's heapq module provides a min-heap on a plain list.
# For max-heap: negate values on insert and negate back on extract.

import heapq

# ── Min-heap via heapq ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
h = []
heapq.heappush(h, 5)   # O(log n) insert
heapq.heappush(h, 1)
heapq.heappush(h, 3)

print(h[0])              # O(1) peek — always the min
print(heapq.heappop(h))  # O(log n) extract min → 1

# O(n) heapify from existing list
arr = [5, 3, 8, 1, 9, 2, 7]
heapq.heapify(arr)       # in-place O(n)
print(arr[0])            # 1

# ── Max-heap — negate values ──────────────────────────────────────────────
max_h = []
for v in [5, 1, 3, 9, 2]:
    heapq.heappush(max_h, -v)   # negate on insert

max_val = -heapq.heappop(max_h) # negate back → 9
print(max_val)  # 9

# ── Custom priority — (priority, value) tuples ───────────────────────────
tasks = []
heapq.heappush(tasks, (2, 'medium task'))
heapq.heappush(tasks, (1, 'urgent task'))
heapq.heappush(tasks, (3, 'low priority'))

priority, task = heapq.heappop(tasks)  # → (1, 'urgent task')

# ── nlargest / nsmallest — O(n log k) ───────────────────────────────────
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]
print(heapq.nlargest(3, nums))   # [9, 6, 5]
print(heapq.nsmallest(3, nums))  # [1, 1, 2]

# ── Manual min-heap class ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
class MinHeap:
    def __init__(self): self.h = []

    def push(self, v):   heapq.heappush(self.h, v)
    def pop(self):       return heapq.heappop(self.h)
    def peek(self):      return self.h[0]
    def size(self):      return len(self.h)
    def is_empty(self):  return len(self.h) == 0

    @classmethod
    def heapify(cls, arr):
        obj = cls()
        obj.h = arr[:]
        heapq.heapify(obj.h)
        return obj

footerAlerts.title: When heaps shine
items: Priority queue — O(log n) insert/extract with O(1) peek. The canonical use case for any heap.
Top-K problems — maintain a size-K heap while scanning n elements: O(n log K) total, far better than sorting O(n log n) when K is small.
Heap sort — O(n log n) in-place, O(1) auxiliary space. Not stable but cache-friendly.
Graph algorithms — Dijkstra and Prim's MST both use a min-heap to greedily pick the closest unvisited node in O((V+E) log V).

footerAlerts.title: Key index formulas (memorise these)
items: Leaf nodes: indices ⌊n/2⌋ to n-1 — no sift-down needed

footerAlerts.title: Common interview pitfalls
items: Confusing min-heap with sorted array — heap is only partially ordered. Inorder traversal does NOT produce sorted output.
Using O(n log n) repeated inserts instead of O(n) heapify when bulk-loading.
Java PriorityQueue is min-heap by default — explicitly use reverseOrder() for max-heap.
Forgetting that heap guarantees O(1) peek but O(log n) for everything else except heapify.

hints: For the median of a stream problem: use a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half — balance sizes to get O(log n) insert and O(1) median.
Heapify is O(n) — always prefer it over n individual inserts when you have all elements upfront.
Delete-at and Update-key are O(log n) each — never O(n). The trick is replacing with the last element and doing a single up or down pass.
